content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

February 7, 1991

John Fellows
City Manager
1 Civic Center Plaza
P.O. Box 19575
Irvine, CA 92713

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-672

Dear Mr. Fellows:

You have requested advice on behalf of City of Irvine Mayor
Sally Anne Sheridan concerning application of the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). The
following advice is based upon the facts provided in the letter
from James R. Sutton of Nielsen, Merksamer, et al.

QUESTION

Under the Act, may Mayor Sheridan participate in a
governmental decision affecting a company if either she or her
husband has represented an employee of the company in a real
estate transaction?

CONCLUSION

Mayor Sheridan may participate in a governmental decision
affecting a company if only the employee/client, and not the
company, is a source of income to her.

FACTS

Mayor Sheridan, a real estate agent in Newport Beach, often
represents individuals in real estate transactions who are
employees of companies which may have matters pending before the
Irvine City Council. The commissions she receives from these
transactions normally exceed $250.

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

File No. A-90-672
Page 2


Mayor Sheridan's husband, also a real estate agent in Newport
Beach, also represents individuals who are employees of companies
which may have matters pending before the Irvine City Council.
His commissions normally exceed $500.

Neither Mayor nor Mr. Sheridan have an ownership interest in
the real estate company for which they work. Likewise, the
employees the Sheridans represent in real estate transactions do
not hold ownership interests in the companies for which they work.

ANALYSIS

The Act prohibits a public official from making, participat-
ing in, or using her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which she knows or has reason to know she has a
financial interest. (Section 87100.) A public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of her immediate family or on, among other
things:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dol-
lars ($250) or more in value provided to, received
by or promised to the public official within 12
months prior to the time when the decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

Section 87103 (a)-(d).

As a public official, Ms. Sheridan may not participate in a
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect on any business entity in which
she has a financial interest or on any person or entity which was
a source of income to her or her husband within 12 months preced-
ing the decision. (Section 87103(a), (c) and (d).)

File No. A-90-672
Page 3

Regulation 18704.3, which defines sources of commission
income for real estate agents, provides in pertinent part:

(c) The sources of commission income in a
specific sale or similar transaction include for
each of the following:

* * *

(3) A real estate agent:

(A) The broker and brokerage busi-
ness entity under whose auspices the
agent works;

(B) The person the agent represents
in the transaction; and

(C) Any person who receives a
finder's or other referral fee for refer-
ring a party to the transaction to the
broker, or who makes a referral pursuant
to a contract with the broker.

Pursuant to the regulation, only the real estate company for
which Mayor Sheridan works and the employee/client that either
Mayor Sheridan or her husband represents, is a source of income to
Mayor Sheridan. The company that the employee/client works for is
not a source of income to Mayor Sheridan, unless the company makes
a referral or receives a finder's fee. (Regulation
18704.3(c)(3)(C).) Therefore, if only the employee/client is a
source of income to Mayor Sheridan, she may participate in
governmental decisions regarding the company for which the
employee/client works.

I trust this answers your questions. If you have any further
questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Jill R. Stecher
Counsel, Legal Division
